does: This paper demonstrates a novel semantic denial-of-service (DoS) attack against language-model-controlled robots, where adversaries inject short safety-plausible phrases (1-5 tokens) into audio channels to trigger false safety reasoning without jailbreaking the model. The authors evaluate the attack across four vision-language models, seven prompt-level defenses, three deployment modes, and both single and multi-injection scenarios. They find that while safety-oriented instruction-following protects against harmful commands, it creates exploitable vulnerabilities where legitimate-sounding alerts cause robots to halt or disrupt execution. The paper introduces Disruption Success Rate (DSR) as a metric and discovers that current prompt-only defenses fundamentally trade off genuine hazard response against attack suppression, often merely transforming hard stops into acknowledge loops and false alerts rather than eliminating disruption.
connects: This work builds on research in adversarial robustness for language models and embodied AI systems, extending prior jailbreaking studies to the availability domain. It responds to growing concerns about safety mechanisms in deployed AI systems, particularly how safeguards designed to prevent harmful behaviors can paradoxically introduce new attack surfaces. The paper relates to literature on prompt injection attacks and instruction-following in multimodal models, showing how safety alignment—a major focus of recent LLM research—creates unintended vulnerabilities in physical systems. It also connects to work on adversarial examples and perturbations in embodied AI, but shifts focus from model evasion to functionality disruption through semantic rather than visual or syntactic manipulation.
enables: This research opens investigation into defenses beyond prompt-level mitigation, motivating deeper architectural changes to how embodied AI systems validate safety-critical inputs. It suggests that detecting out-of-distribution safety signals, implementing multi-modal consistency checks, or redesigning robot decision-making to be more robust to contradictory alerts could mitigate the attack. The work points toward formal verification approaches for safety policies in embodied systems and raises questions about how to maintain genuine hazard responsiveness while filtering malicious inputs. Future work might explore whether architectural hardening, temporal consistency analysis, or specialized training on adversarial safety phrases could resolve the fundamental defense trade-off the paper identifies, ultimately informing safer deployment practices for autonomous systems in shared environments.
